attack_chain.description: Retrieve exposed IAM credentials from the publicly accessible bait S3 bucket. The terraform.tfvars file containing the victim access key ID and secret is downloaded anonymously via a public bucket policy, simulating a Codefinger-style actor scanning misconfigured IaC state buckets for harvested IAM keys (T1552.001 discovery vector executed pre-authentication).

implementation.method: Anonymous boto3 S3 client (no credentials) performs GetObject on the bait bucket to retrieve the exposed credential file

api_calls.note: Executed without AWS credentials — codefinger-bait-public-bucket-policy allows anonymous s3:GetObject on this key only

implementation.post_processing: Parse returned file body for aws_access_key_id and aws_secret_access_key; store as stolen_key_id and stolen_secret for boto3 session construction in step 2

detection_sources: CloudTrail S3 data-plane events on bait bucket
cleanup_actions: Read-only retrieval of existing object — no resources created
iocs_generated: Anonymous GetObject request to bait bucket from external IP
boto3 user-agent string with no AWS credential signature

attack_chain.description: Construct a boto3 session using the stolen long-term IAM access key and secret recovered in step 1. Validate the stolen credentials via GetCallerIdentity, then survey all accessible S3 buckets via ListBuckets to identify the target — replicating Codefinger's initial authentication and environment reconnaissance posture.

implementation.method: boto3.Session constructed with stolen_key_id and stolen_secret; STS and S3 API calls confirm identity and enumerate bucket targets

cleanup_actions: boto3 session terminates on script exit — no resources created
iocs_generated: API calls originating from novel source IP using long-term IAM key
GetCallerIdentity probe — high-fidelity attacker reconnaissance signal
ListBuckets from IP outside expected access baseline

attack_chain.description: Enumerate all objects in the target bucket across the finance/ and hr/ prefixes using paginated ListObjectsV2. HeadObject calls collect per-object metadata (size, ETag, storage class) to plan the encryption loop. A sample GetObject per prefix confirms read access to actual data content before committing to the encryption phase.

implementation.method: Paginated ListObjectsV2 across full bucket; HeadObject on each discovered key; GetObject on one representative key per prefix to verify data access

api_calls.note: Paginate with continuation token until all keys collected; build object_manifest list for encryption loop
Collect ContentLength, ETag, ServerSideEncryption state; repeat per object
Confirms data-plane read access; object body used in step 4 re-upload — content not exfiltrated

iocs_generated: High-volume ListObjects + HeadObject calls from non-native source IP
GetObject access spanning multiple prefixes in rapid succession
Unusual principal (victim IAM user) accessing data-plane APIs outside business hours

attack_chain.description: SIMULATED: Generate a local AES-256 SSE-C key (the attacker-held key Codefinger uses to hold data hostage). For each synthetic object collected in step 3, retrieve the plaintext body then re-upload it to the same S3 key with SSE-C headers — replacing the plaintext object with an attacker-encrypted copy. CloudTrail logs the HMAC of the SSE-C key but never the key material itself. Deposit a ransom note (README.txt) in each affected prefix (finance/, hr/).

implementation.method: os.urandom(32) generates the AES-256 SSE-C key locally; GetObject retrieves plaintext body; PutObject re-uploads with SSECustomerAlgorithm/SSECustomerKey/SSECustomerKeyMD5; separate PutObject drops ransom note per prefix
implementation.pre_step: import os, base64, hashlib; aes_key = os.urandom(32); aes_key_b64 = base64.b64encode(aes_key).decode(); aes_key_md5 = base64.b64encode(hashlib.md5(aes_key).digest()).decode(); save aes_key to local file for cleanup

api_calls.note: Retrieve plaintext body; repeat per object in object_manifest
Overwrites original with SSE-C encrypted copy; CloudTrail logs HMAC only — attacker retains sole decryption capability; repeat per object

args.Body: Your files have been encrypted with a key only we possess. Send payment to [Bitcoin address] within 7 days or your data will be permanently deleted. Contact: codefinger@proton.me
Your files have been encrypted with a key only we possess. Send payment to [Bitcoin address] within 7 days or your data will be permanently deleted. Contact: codefinger@proton.me

implementation.simulation_note: All objects are synthetic test data generated by Pulumi. The AES-256 SSE-C key is written to local disk immediately for use in cleanup. No production data is involved.

detection_sources: CloudTrail S3 data-plane events (SSE-C PutObject — x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-algorithm header logged)
Macie policy findings on encryption-state change
cleanup_actions: SSE-C encrypted objects deleted in step 5 cleanup path
README.txt ransom notes deleted in cleanup_manifest after pulumi destroy
iocs_generated: PutObject calls with SSE-C headers on objects that previously had no customer-managed encryption
In-place overwrite pattern: PutObject to existing keys at high volume in short window
README.txt objects appearing in multiple prefixes simultaneously
CloudTrail SSE-C HMAC entries — attacker key fingerprint

attack_chain.description: SIMULATED: Delete the original plaintext objects (now overwritten with SSE-C copies). Set a lifecycle deletion policy on the bucket with a 1-day expiry (simulating Codefinger's 7-day TTL — shortened per operational_notes for safe emulation). The lifecycle rule is immediately removed via DeleteBucketLifecycle after CloudTrail captures the PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration event, preventing actual automated deletion of the synthetic test objects.

implementation.method: DeleteObject per original key (already SSE-C overwritten — deletion removes the last plaintext restore path); PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration sets auto-delete rule; DeleteBucketLifecycle removes it immediately after log capture

api_calls.note: Objects were overwritten with SSE-C copies in step 4; DeleteObject removes any unversioned delete markers and ensures original content is not recoverable without attacker key; repeat per object in object_manifest
Removes lifecycle rule immediately after CloudTrail captures PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration — prevents automated deletion of synthetic test data

detection_sources: CloudTrail management events (PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration — high-signal control-plane change)
cleanup_actions: Lifecycle rule deleted in-step immediately after CloudTrail capture — no residual policy
SSE-C encrypted objects remain and are cleaned in cleanup_manifest
iocs_generated: PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration with 1-day expiry applied to a bucket with no prior lifecycle policy
Bulk DeleteObject API calls immediately following high-volume PutObject (SSE-C) activity in same session
Lifecycle rule ID 'codefinger-auto-delete' matching known threat actor naming pattern

attack_chain.description: Suspend S3 object versioning on the target bucket to eliminate version-rollback as an incident response path. Enumerate all existing object versions and delete markers using ListObjectVersions, then bulk-delete them via DeleteObjects with VersionId — permanently foreclosing restoration from prior unencrypted versions. This is the terminal technique in Codefinger's kill chain, completing the ransomware scenario by removing the most common AWS recovery mechanism.

implementation.method: GetBucketVersioning reads current state; PutBucketVersioning suspends versioning; paginated ListObjectVersions collects all VersionIds and DeleteMarkers; DeleteObjects batch-deletes all version entries

api_calls.note: Log current versioning state before modification for cleanup fidelity
Paginate to collect all VersionId values for both versions and delete markers
Batch up to 1000 version entries per call; repeat for delete markers; eliminates all restore points

detection_sources: CloudTrail management events (PutBucketVersioning — disabling versioning is a critical high-fidelity detection signal)
Security Hub S3.14 control (versioning disabled alert)
EventBridge rule on PutBucketVersioning for automated response
cleanup_actions: Re-enable versioning via PutBucketVersioning Status=Enabled before pulumi destroy (listed in cleanup_manifest)
iocs_generated: PutBucketVersioning transitioning bucket from Enabled to Suspended immediately following encryption activity
Bulk DeleteObjectVersion calls on a previously versioned bucket
Complete kill chain signature: SSE-C PutObject + DeleteObject + PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration + PutBucketVersioning + DeleteObjectVersion in single IAM session

credential_chain.source: codefinger-exposed-credentials-object in codefinger-bait-public-bucket — terraform.tfvars file retrieved anonymously via public s3:GetObject, containing victim IAM access key ID and secret (simulates T1552.001 discovery of credentials committed to publicly accessible IaC state storage)
credential_chain.expiration: Manual rotation required — no automatic expiry on long-term IAM access keys

script_manifest.description: Single unified attack script implementing the full Codefinger ransomware kill chain. Reads victim IAM credentials from the public bait bucket anonymously, constructs one persistent boto3 session, and executes all five MITRE techniques in sequence: credential validation (T1078.004), data enumeration (T1530), SSE-C re-encryption simulation (T1486), lifecycle-and-deletion simulation (T1485), and versioning suspension with version purge (T1490). The AES-256 SSE-C key is generated at runtime and written to disk for cleanup use.

phases.name: Initial Access — Credential Harvesting and Validation

attack_created_resources.name: All re-uploaded synthetic objects in codefinger-target-s3-bucket encrypted with attacker AES-256 SSE-C key
ssec_key.bin — AES-256 SSE-C key written to attack host disk

cleanup_manifest.pulumi_managed_resources: All infrastructure destroyed by pulumi destroy — includes both S3 buckets, IAM user/key/policy, CloudTrail trail and log bucket
cleanup_order: Re-enable S3 versioning on codefinger-target-s3-bucket: PutBucketVersioning Status=Enabled
Delete SSE-C encrypted synthetic objects in codefinger-target-s3-bucket — objects cannot be read back without ssec_key.bin but can be deleted without the key via DeleteObject
Delete ransom note objects: finance/README.txt and hr/README.txt from codefinger-target-s3-bucket
Verify lifecycle configuration is absent on codefinger-target-s3-bucket (deleted in-step during T1485)
Securely delete local ssec_key.bin from attack host
